problem_summary: 2025 MCM Problem B (sustainable tourism / "managing overtourism"), discrete-type. The task: build a model to balance tourist volume, revenue, and resource conservation for Juneau, Alaska; recommend management strategies; do sensitivity analysis; and generalize to another destination. This team (#2503268) framed it as a three-dimensional system — population (tourists), environment (glacier health via Equilibrium Line Altitude), economy (hotel tax revenue) — coupled by ODEs, then layered an AHP scoring model on top and a discrete strategy-search that selects the best intervention combination. They optimized a sustainability Score from 0.446 to 0.692 for Juneau, and validated transfer to Big Sur, California (0.328 to 0.736).
models_used: Sustainable Tourism Dynamics Model (STDM): a coupled 3-ODE system over H (glacier ELA height), N (tourist number), T (hotel tax revenue), explicitly inspired by Lotka-Volterra, the Verhulst logistic model, and the SIS epidemic model
Logistic/Verhulst growth term r1*N*(1-N/K) for tourist-number dynamics with carrying capacity K
Lotka-Volterra-style coupling terms (k2*H*N environmental degradation from tourism, k4*H*N glacier-health boosts demand, k3*T*H conservation investment) — predator-prey-like interaction structure
Nonlinear regression / least-squares parameter fitting of k_i and r_i on 2010-2019 historical data, with additive error terms epsilon to absorb unmodeled shocks
Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP): 4 pairwise-comparison judgment matrices, eigenvalue weights, consistency check CR<0.1, hierarchical aggregation of 3 factors x 4 sub-indicators (12 indicators total)

Discrete intervention-strategy search: enumerate 9 tax-allocation vectors [mu1..mu5] x 8 policy vectors [v1,v2,v3], evaluate each combo through STDM + AHP under constraints, pick the max-Score combination (greedy per-step optimization)
Morris method (global / qualitative sensitivity) — mu*-sigma diagram on ODE params and on evaluation-function params
Local sensitivity analysis (quantitative) — partial derivatives dScore/dp_i ranked by mean absolute value with std across mu settings

Bespoke indicator scoring functions: sigmoid saturation (crowding, carbon, energy, natural resources), power-law (noise), logarithmic smoothing (income), min/floor penalty (waste), min-max normalization
key_techniques: Reducing a fuzzy sustainability problem to three measurable proxy indicators (ELA for environment, tourist count for population, hotel tax for economy) and justifying each proxy choice explicitly (e.g., tourists >> residents so locals are negligible in the population dimension; hotel tax is a stable share of government revenue)

Choosing glacier Equilibrium Line Altitude (ELA) as the environmental state variable — a real glaciology indicator (Braithwaite, Oerlemans) tying the model to a concrete, paper-specific Juneau feature (Mendenhall Glacier)

Two-layer architecture: a dynamical layer (ODEs predict state evolution) feeding a decision layer (AHP scores states+strategies), with the Score feeding back to pick the next strategy — a closed optimization loop

Encoding policies as constraint vectors with a conservation invariant (-v1+v2+v3=10, with H treated as a negative indicator) so 8 qualitatively different policies become quantitatively comparable inputs

Data hygiene as a narrative asset: explicitly excluding the 2013 'Blob' marine-heatwave anomaly and interpolating, then citing COVID-2020 as out-of-scope — turning a data problem into a credibility signal
Dual sensitivity analysis (Morris global + local partial-derivative ranking) cross-validating each other (k2, k6, r1, k5 flagged by both)

Generalization by parameter swap: replacing glacier ELA with coastal-landslide/cliff-retreat height for Big Sur while keeping the same ODE structure, with destination-specific literature backing the new proxy
Quantified before/after Score deltas as headline evidence of impact (0.446->0.692 Juneau; 0.328->0.736 Big Sur; baseline-vs-model bar chart)
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on integration and storytelling, not on any single advanced method. It does three things O-papers reliably do: (1) it commits to a concrete, defensible problem reduction — three named proxies with explicit justification — so every later equation is traceable to a real quantity; (2) it builds a genuinely coupled system where the sub-models feed each other (ODE state -> AHP score -> strategy choice -> next ODE state), which reads as one model rather than three bolted-together pieces; and (3) it closes the loop with quantified, decision-ready output (a single Score the government can move from 0.45 to 0.69, plus a concrete memo recommending a 15,000/day cap and 40% revenue to conservation). The ELA/glacier framing is memorable and locally specific (Mendenhall), which judges reward over generic 'environment index' treatments. The dual sensitivity analysis that self-cross-validates, the honest exclusion of the 2013 Blob anomaly, and the clean Big Sur transfer (re-deriving an environmental proxy from cited coastal-erosion literature rather than hand-waving) all signal rigor and self-awareness. It is comprehensive, readable, well-cited (11 real references), and ends with an actionable memo — the full MCM package.
section_highlights: Front matter: a strong one-page summary that names the three proxies, the three source models, and the headline Score deltas up front, followed immediately by a stakeholder memo with specific numbers (15,000 visitors/day cap, 40% revenue to conservation -> ~30% slower glacier degradation). Model Design (Sec 5): each ODE term is introduced one at a time with a cited justification (Braithwaite/Oerlemans for ELA self-feedback, Verhulst for logistic growth, Buckley for environment->demand, Frechtling for baseline economic growth) before assembling the full system — pedagogically clean. Sec 5.2: intervention strategies cleanly tabulated as 9 allocation vectors + 8 policy vectors with a conservation invariant. Evaluation Model (Sec 6): 12 indicator formulas each with a one-paragraph rationale plus a full AHP table (eigenvalues, CI, CR<0.1, weights). Sensitivity (Sec 9): Morris mu*-sigma plus a full local-sensitivity table ranking all ~36 parameters. Big Sur (Sec 10): genuine generalization with a re-derived environmental proxy and its own cited sources. Honest Strengths/Weaknesses naming the no-shock and instantaneous-policy assumptions, and a transparent AI-use appendix showing ChatGPT was used only for language polishing.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The STDM is qualitatively plausible but quantitatively shaky: H, N, T are fit on only ~9 annual points (2010-2019) with up to 6 parameters per equation, so the model is almost certainly over-parameterized/over-fit; no train/test split, cross-validation, or confidence intervals are reported

The reproduced figure data shows the T (tax revenue) model fit visibly diverging from observed data after ~step 8 (model flat ~0.74-0.88 vs observed ~0.91-1.00), yet the text claims a 'strong' fit — the validation is asserted more than demonstrated

Many evaluation-model parameters are admittedly 'experience-based' or 'known data times a ratio' (Sec 6.3) — i.e., hand-tuned to make scores 'reasonable', which makes the final Score partly arbitrary and weakens the 0.446->0.692 headline

The discrete strategy search is only 9x8=72 fixed combinations chosen greedily per step; it is not a true optimization over the policy space and could miss better continuous allocations; 'optimal' is optimal only within this small menu

Internal inconsistencies/sloppiness: notation drift (k7-k11 appear in sensitivity tables but the ODEs only define k1-k6; the Morris text references 'k10'), the local-sensitivity table has duplicate rank numbers and mixed scales, and several constants (P=31685 vs P=31685 used as Juneau population) are under-explained

ELA as 'glacier health' is inverted and loosely used — rising ELA means worse health, but it is sometimes described as a positive 'health' variable; the sign conventions in S-indicators (H as negative indicator) are easy to misread
Self-acknowledged: no capacity for shocks (Blob, COVID) and assumes instantaneous policy implementation with no lag — both materially affect a tourism system

Big Sur 'validation' reuses the same fitted k-structure but the dramatic 0.328->0.736 jump is again driven by the same hand-tuned scoring layer, so it demonstrates transferability of the framework more than predictive accuracy
reusable_patterns: Proxy-reduction pattern: for any vague multi-objective sustainability/'balance' problem, pick ONE measurable state variable per dimension and write an explicit justification paragraph for each choice — this anchors the whole paper and is highly transferable

Two-layer dynamics+decision architecture: use coupled ODEs (Lotka-Volterra/logistic/SIS as building blocks) for the 'how the system evolves' layer, and AHP (or TOPSIS/entropy weighting) for the 'how good is this state' scoring layer, then close the loop by feeding the score back into a discrete strategy chooser
Strategy-as-vector pattern: encode qualitative policy options as numeric vectors with a conservation/sum invariant so heterogeneous interventions become comparable, enumerable inputs to the model
Headline-delta evidence: report a single scalar objective and show before->after improvement plus a baseline-vs-model comparison chart — gives judges an instantly graspable impact claim
Dual sensitivity analysis as standard practice: pair Morris global (mu*-sigma) with local partial-derivative ranking and show they agree — cheap, rigorous-looking, and self-validating

Anomaly-exclusion narrative: identify a named real-world shock in the data (here the 2013 Blob), exclude+interpolate it, and state out-of-scope shocks (COVID) — converts data limitations into a credibility signal

Generalization-by-proxy-swap: keep the ODE/scoring skeleton fixed and only re-derive the destination-specific environmental proxy from cited local literature — a clean, defensible template for the 'apply to another location' sub-question
Stakeholder memo with concrete numbers: translate model output into 2-3 specific, quantified recommendations addressed to the named decision-maker
